selected_paper.title: Finding Deceptive Opinion Spam by Any Stretch of the Imagination
selected_paper.venue: Proceedings of the 49th Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics (ACL 2011)

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: PHASE 1 — Understanding all Part A requirements before starting paper search
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: here is the attached PDF of a open exam for my mid semester exam in Advance Machine learning subject of 6th semester, I want your help to complete this open exam. First analyse the attached PDF thoroughly word by word then explain all the instructions and procedure to complete this exam
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Gave a complete breakdown of all Part A requirements: CORE A* venue constraint, 2009-2012 year range, SVM/GMM/ARIMA method alignment, dataset availability rules, CPU-only compute requirement, JSON structure for disclosure, and GitHub submission format. This gave me a clear checklist before I started searching for any paper.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: I re-read the original exam PDF section by section and confirmed every point Claude extracted was accurate. I also noted the 50-mark rubric breakdown myself to understand which criteria carry the most weight.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: PHASE 1 — Identifying which topic (SVM/GMM/ARIMA) would be easiest to implement and reproduce
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: No preference — suggest the easiest to reproduce among ARIMA, GMM and SVM
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Claude explained that SVM is the safest choice because scikit-learn has a battle-tested LinearSVC implementation, text datasets are small and CPU-friendly, and the theory is well understood and explainable in Part C without complex math. This focused my entire search on SVM papers only.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: I verified this independently by checking scikit-learn documentation for SVM and comparing the implementation complexity against GMM (requires EM convergence debugging) and ARIMA (requires time-series stationarity checks). SVM was clearly simplest.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: PHASE 2 — Checking which papers are already taken by classmates to avoid duplicate disqualification
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: here is the excel sheets of current student paper selection and its details
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Claude identified all already-taken papers and their venues, helping me avoid wasting time on unavailable papers. This prevented automatic disqualification under the first-come-first-select rule.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: I personally opened the live Google Sheets response sheet and cross-checked every paper title to confirm the analysis was accurate.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: PHASE 2 — Evaluating whether the sarcasm detection paper qualifies under all exam constraints
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: I want you to analyse the qualification of the paper i have selected according to the requirements mentioned in the Mid Sem Exam requirement PDF, here is the paper details i have chosen:- Title:- Identifying Sarcasm in Twitter: A Closer Look Paper Link:- https://aclanthology.org/P11-2102.pdf
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Confirmed the ACL 2011 venue and SVM method alignment were valid, but flagged that the original Twitter dataset may not be publicly available — a critical reproducibility risk worth 5 marks.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: Independently searched Google for the dataset and confirmed no official download link exists for the original 2011 sarcasm Twitter corpus, validating the concern raised.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: PHASE 2 — Verifying dataset availability for the sarcasm paper before committing to it
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: the dataset used in this paper is available right? where is the link
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Claude confirmed the original Twitter sarcasm dataset was never publicly released by the authors, making the paper risky for reproduction. This directly triggered the decision to search for a paper with a guaranteed freely downloadable exact dataset.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: Searched independently and found no author page, no GitHub, no official release of the dataset. Confirmed Claude's finding was accurate.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: PHASE 2 — Evaluating the word vectors paper and checking if SVM qualifies as its primary method
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: okay i have changed the paper after reviewing the dataset issue. now review this paper if it is fulfilling the requirements mentioned in Exam PDF. Here are the details of new research paper:- Paper Name:- Learning Word Vectors for Sentiment Analysis Paper PDF link:- https://aclanthology.org/P11-1015.pdf
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Gemini flagged that the primary contribution of this paper is word vector learning (representation learning), not SVM classification. SVM is only used as an evaluation baseline. This would have cost me 4 marks for method alignment and risked disqualification.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: Read the abstract of Maas et al. 2011 on ACL Anthology and confirmed the primary contribution is explicitly described as learning word vectors, not SVM. Validated the warning independently.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: PHASE 2 — Full qualification check of the deceptive opinion spam paper against every exam requirement
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: okay i have again changed the paper after reviewing the previous Experimental scope issue. now review this paper if it is fulfilling the requirements mentioned in Exam PDF. Here are the details of new research paper:- Paper Name:- Finding Deceptive Opinion Spam by Any Stretch of the Imagination Paper PDF link:- https://aclanthology.org/P11-1032.pdf
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Confirmed all criteria are met: ACL 2011 (CORE A*), SVM is the primary method throughout, exact original dataset freely available at myleott.com, CPU-only feasible, and clearly methodological (three SVM approaches compared). This was the green light to proceed with this paper.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: Visited myleott.com/op_spam_v1.4.zip and confirmed immediate download without login. Read the paper abstract to verify SVM is used throughout as the primary classifier, not a baseline.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: PHASE 3 — Understanding the core research question and what problem this paper actually solves
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: I have selected the paper Finding Deceptive Opinion Spam by Any Stretch of the Imagination by Ott et al. 2011. Can you explain what exact problem this paper is trying to solve and why it is hard for humans to detect fake reviews manually?
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Explained that the paper addresses the problem of detecting fake hotel reviews written by paid workers, and that human judges perform at only 60% accuracy (near random chance) because they cannot consciously track subtle statistical patterns across thousands of word features simultaneously. This gave me a clear mental model of why ML is needed here.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: Confirmed by reading the introduction and Section 4 of the paper, which explicitly reports human judge accuracy at 57-62% across different conditions, compared to the SVM classifier at 89%.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: PHASE 3 — Understanding the three SVM feature approaches and what makes each one different
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: In the Ott et al. 2011 paper, the authors compare three different SVM classifiers. Can you explain clearly what features each one uses, how they differ from each other, and which one performs best and why?
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Clarified the three approaches: (1) n-gram SVM using unigram and bigram word frequencies via TF-IDF, (2) LIWC-SVM using 80 psycholinguistic categories like spatial language, family references, and emotional words, (3) POS-SVM using part-of-speech tag frequencies. The n-gram SVM performs best because word frequency patterns in short text are the most discriminative signal for deception detection.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: Verified by reading Table 2 in the paper which reports accuracy scores for each feature set. Unigrams+bigrams achieved the highest accuracy consistent with Claude's explanation.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: PHASE 3 — Understanding the key linguistic finding about what makes deceptive reviews different from truthful ones
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: What are the main linguistic differences between deceptive and truthful hotel reviews according to Ott et al. 2011? What specific word patterns does the SVM pick up on?
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Explained that deceptive reviews use more imaginative, sensory, and emotionally charged language (words like 'amazing', 'husband', 'vacation', 'business trip') while truthful reviews use more specific, factual, and spatial language (words like 'bathroom', 'location', 'small', 'check-in'). This insight is central to understanding why the SVM works and is essential for Part C.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: Confirmed by reading Section 5.1 of the paper where the authors analyse the most discriminative features and discuss the psycholinguistic interpretation of deceptive versus truthful writing styles.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: PHASE 3 — Understanding the key assumptions made in this paper that could be questioned in Part C
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: What are the main assumptions that Ott et al. 2011 make in their experimental setup that could be considered limitations or weaknesses? I want to be able to discuss these critically in Part C.
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Identified four key assumptions: (1) balanced 50/50 dataset which is unrealistic in real-world fraud detection, (2) Mechanical Turk workers are not professional spammers so the model may not generalise, (3) only positive reviews are in the first version of the corpus, (4) domain is hotel reviews only and cross-domain performance is unknown.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: Found all four points discussed in the paper's limitations section and conclusion. These are exactly the kinds of critical observations the professor will look for in Part C.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: PHASE 4 — Planning the exact reproduction pipeline for Part B
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: If I need to reproduce the Ott et al. 2011 paper results for Part B of my exam using Python and scikit-learn on a CPU-only laptop, what is the exact step by step pipeline I should follow? What libraries do I need and what accuracy should I expect?
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Provided the complete reproduction pipeline: download dataset from myleott.com, load text files, apply TfidfVectorizer with ngram_range=(1,2) and norm='l2', train LinearSVC with C=1.0, evaluate using 5-fold cross_val_score. Expected accuracy 85-89%, matching the paper's reported results. Total code under 25 lines.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: Tested the pipeline locally. The dataset loaded correctly from the zip file. TfidfVectorizer and LinearSVC from sklearn ran in under 10 seconds. Got 87.3% accuracy on 5-fold CV which falls within the expected range.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: PHASE 4 — Understanding how 5-fold cross-validation works in the context of this specific dataset
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: The Ott et al. 2011 paper uses nested 5-fold cross-validation. The dataset is split into 5 folds by hotel. Can you explain why they split by hotel rather than randomly, and what would go wrong if I just used random splits?
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Explained that splitting by hotel prevents data leakage — if reviews from the same hotel appear in both train and test, the model learns hotel-specific writing styles rather than general deception patterns. Random splits would inflate accuracy artificially. This is a subtle but important methodological point I need to understand for Part B.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: Inspected the dataset folder structure and confirmed reviews are organised by hotel (fold_1 to fold_5 within each hotel folder), consistent with the paper's description of their cross-validation setup.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: PHASE 5 — Preparing for Part C by understanding how to explain SVM mechanics on this task without any tools
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: For my proctored exam Part C, I need to explain without internet how an SVM takes sparse TF-IDF text features from hotel reviews, maps them into high-dimensional space, and draws a decision boundary to separate deceptive from truthful reviews. Can you give me a simple intuitive explanation I can memorise and reconstruct from first principles?
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Gave a clean three-step explanation: (1) each review becomes a sparse vector where each dimension is a word's TF-IDF weight, (2) the SVM finds a hyperplane in this high-dimensional space that maximises the margin between deceptive and truthful review clusters, (3) for a new review its vector is computed and the side of the hyperplane it falls on determines the prediction. This is the exact explanation I can reconstruct without tools.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: Practised explaining this from memory without referring to any notes. Was able to reconstruct the full explanation coherently. Also linked it back to the specific features in the paper to make the explanation concrete.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: PHASE 5 — Preparing specific failure case analysis for Part C
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: What are the specific cases where the SVM classifier in Ott et al. 2011 would fail to correctly classify a review? I want concrete, paper-grounded failure cases I can discuss in the proctored exam.
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Identified five concrete failure cases: (1) professional spammers who mimic truthful writing patterns, (2) sarcastic genuine reviews that use exaggerated positive language, (3) cross-domain failure when the model trained on hotels is applied to restaurants or products, (4) class imbalance in real-world data where fake reviews are far rarer than 50%, (5) short reviews under 10 words where TF-IDF features are too sparse for reliable classification.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: Checked each failure case against the paper. Cases 1, 3, and 4 are explicitly discussed in the paper's limitations. Cases 2 and 5 are reasonable extensions I verified by thinking through the SVM decision boundary geometry myself.

top_5_prompts.prompt: In the Ott et al. 2011 paper, the authors compare three different SVM classifiers. Can you explain clearly what features each one uses, how they differ from each other, and which one performs best and why?
top_5_prompts.why_important: This was the most analytically important prompt of my entire process because it forced me to understand the paper's actual technical contribution at a deep level. Simply knowing 'the paper uses SVM' is not enough — understanding WHY the authors chose three different feature sets, what each one captures about human writing behaviour, and why unigrams outperform psycholinguistic features is the core intellectual content of the paper. This understanding is what will carry me through Part C without any tools.

top_5_prompts.prompt: For my proctored exam Part C, I need to explain without internet how an SVM takes sparse TF-IDF text features from hotel reviews, maps them into high-dimensional space, and draws a decision boundary to separate deceptive from truthful reviews. Can you give me a simple intuitive explanation I can memorise and reconstruct from first principles?
top_5_prompts.why_important: Part C is proctored with no internet. The ability to explain SVM mechanics from first principles — sparse vectors, high-dimensional space, margin maximisation, support vectors — entirely from memory is not optional. This prompt directly prepared me for that requirement. The explanation Claude provided is exactly what I practised memorising and can now reconstruct independently.

top_5_prompts.prompt: What are the main assumptions that Ott et al. 2011 make in their experimental setup that could be considered limitations or weaknesses? I want to be able to discuss these critically in Part C.
top_5_prompts.why_important: Critical evaluation of a paper's assumptions is a higher-order analytical skill that distinguishes surface-level understanding from genuine mastery. The exam explicitly tests failure cases and limitations. Identifying the four core assumptions — balanced dataset, Mechanical Turk workers, positive reviews only, single domain — gave me concrete, paper-grounded points to raise in Part C that show I read the paper critically rather than just accepting its results.

top_5_prompts.prompt: The Ott et al. 2011 paper uses nested 5-fold cross-validation. The dataset is split into 5 folds by hotel. Can you explain why they split by hotel rather than randomly, and what would go wrong if I just used random splits?
top_5_prompts.why_important: This prompt revealed a subtle but important methodological detail that most students would miss. Understanding why hotel-based splits prevent data leakage — and what inflated accuracy would look like with random splits — demonstrates genuine understanding of experimental design rather than just copying code. This is exactly the kind of question the professor might ask in Part C to distinguish students who truly understand their paper from those who just ran it.

top_5_prompts.prompt: the dataset used in this paper is available right? where is the link
top_5_prompts.why_important: This was the pivotal decision-making prompt that redirected my entire paper selection strategy. Discovering that the original Twitter sarcasm dataset was never publicly released taught me that dataset availability must be verified before committing to any paper — not assumed. This directly led me to prioritise the Ott et al. 2011 paper, whose exact original dataset is freely available from the author's own page. Without this prompt I would have submitted a paper I could never fully reproduce.

student_declaration.statement: I declare that this JSON file contains a complete and honest record of my LLM usage for Part A. I used Claude (Anthropic) and Gemini (Google) across five research phases: understanding exam requirements, paper search and selection, deep paper analysis, reproduction planning, and Part C preparation. All final decisions, verifications, and understanding are my own. I have read the paper, tested the reproduction pipeline locally, and am fully prepared to explain the methodology independently in Part C without any tools.
